serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of linguine aglio olio with prawns and nduja is approximately 350 g, comprising a full plate of pasta with tiger prawns and nduja sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pasta, cooked, enriched, without added salt (FDC ID 20420) combined with component estimates for olive oil, prawns, and nduja
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: cooked linguine (~55% by weight, USDA FDC 20420), tiger prawns (~20%, USDA FDC 175180), extra virgin olive oil (~10%, USDA FDC 171413), nduja (~10%, estimated from USDA FDC salami/pork sausage data FDC 167877), garlic and chili (~5%, USDA FDC 169230 and 170931). Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for a restaurant-style preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Pasta, linguine, cooked (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for linguine aglio olio with prawns and nduja. GI of 46 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked linguine pasta (GI ~46, low category). The addition of olive oil, prawns, and nduja does not substantially raise the GI; fat and protein content may modestly lower the effective GI. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (46 × 75.25) / 100 ≈ 34.6, rounded to 34.8 using 350 g serving × 21.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 75.25 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from nduja and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each serving provides approximately 1120 mg sodium, primarily from nduja and added salt; individuals with hypertension should request reduced seasoning and limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined pasta carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glycemic load per serving is approximately 35, driven by refined linguine; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial glucose response, as the olive oil and protein from prawns partially attenuate the glycemic rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from nduja and overall sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Nduja contributes saturated fat (~1.8 g per 100 g) and sodium to this dish; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and choose preparations with reduced nduja quantity, while benefiting from the cardioprotective monounsaturated fats in extra virgin olive oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Linguine Aglio Olio with Prawns and Nduja is an Italian pasta dish featuring long, flat linguine noodles tossed in a sauce of extra virgin olive oil, thinly sliced garlic, fresh chili, plump tiger prawns, and nduja — a spreadable, intensely spicy Calabrian pork salami paste. The pasta is boiled al dente, then sautéed in the fragrant garlic-chili oil before the prawns are added and cooked until just pink. Nduja is stirred through at the end, melting into the oil to create a rich, spicy, umami-laden sauce that coats each strand of pasta. The dish delivers a bold combination of heat, brininess from the prawns, and deep pork-fat richness from the nduja, balanced by the grassy notes of quality olive oil. Texturally, the linguine is firm and silky, the prawns are tender and juicy, and the sauce is glossy and lightly emulsified. Nutritionally, this dish provides moderate energy (~195 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein from tiger prawns (~9.2 g per 100 g), and heart-healthy monounsaturated fats from olive oil. The sodium content is elevated due to nduja and seasoning (~320 mg per 100 g), making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is low (~46) owing to the pasta matrix, though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate (~35). This dish is not suitable for halal or kosher diets due to pork content, and contains gluten and crustacean allergens. It is commonly served as a main course in Italian restaurants in Singapore, including at Ricciotti - The Riverwalk.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked pasta, tiger prawns, extra virgin olive oil, and pork salami. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked linguine. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are component-estimated composites; no direct approved-source entry for this exact dish exists. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as vitamin D is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used as component sources for this composite dish (FDC IDs 20420, 175180, 171413, 167877, 169230, 170931); vitamin D data is absent from these records and is therefore genuinely unknown for this preparation. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero based on the absence of partially hydrogenated oils in the ingredient profile (olive oil and fresh ingredients). monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g values are estimated primarily from the olive oil component (USDA FDC 171413). Sodium estimate of 320 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant-level seasoning including nduja salt content; actual values may vary by preparation. The cuisine tag 'Chicken Dish' in the input record is erroneous and has been removed; this dish contains no chicken. The food_name has been standardised to 'Linguine Aglio Olio with Prawns and Nduja' as the canonical English form. This dish is not halal due to pork (nduja) content. GI category is low (GI = 46, which is ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules).

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full record with component-estimated nutrients from USDA FDC. Identified that Italian cuisine does not require Singaporean multilingual set under G6; Chinese names added as supplementary context only.
Iteration 2: Addressed g3_numeric_hygiene hard gate failure by adding documentation for vitamin_d_mcg = -1 in the notes field. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list.

Iteration 3: Strengthened g3_numeric_hygiene compliance by explicitly naming all USDA FDC component source IDs (20420, 175180, 171413, 167877, 169230, 170931) in the notes field justification for vitamin_d_mcg = -1, confirming that vitamin D is not reported in any of these source records and is therefore genuinely unknown for this composite preparation. This satisfies the sentinel value documentation rule requiring explicit reason and source for every -1 value.
